I already got down. Yeah, and so any kind of new member questionnaire, pre-seminar question, and again, you can't see even this group. So what would you guys pay to be here? I don't remember. I don't know. It's like 12 Gs or something, right? There's at least four of you who didn't fill them out. And there's people who didn't get their homework in. And there's people who sent their homework in like 48 hours ago, which it's laying in a heap somewhere. So by the way, it doesn't matter how much money you take from them. You never get 100% involvement unless you go sit there and make them do it. So it's the hardest of all of this. But it is a way you can deliver value without having to do anything. Sometimes we're really trying to get beyond just influence. We want loyalty. We want them all to put on our funny hats and march out into the world and drag people back to us. We want them to be online defending us in forums where people talk about us. That kind of behavior retention is certainly for many of you, maybe most of you, for us the biggest issue of the day is not just getting them, but keeping them. And how can we keep them for a month longer, three months longer, a year longer? How can we keep them in the game? And I have a lot to say about that as we go along. And then last, I think, would be ascension, that we write in order to get them to move up, meaning actual ascension ladders, which many of you have in your business, meaning that they go to the next rung and give you more money, and go to the next rung and give you more money. But at the same time, their emotional commitment to the entire process goes up. So those are the things that we're trying to achieve with what we do. And I guess my umbrella point is that it's complicated. If you just take this as a checklist, and now you go back and pick a piece of your writing, pick anything you want, pick last month's newsletter you did, pick the last book you wrote, pick the last article you sent out, pick last week's emails you sent out, and see how many of these things you can find covered in the individual piece of writing, you will see this thing ain't easy. But that's exactly part of the game, is trying to touch all the necessary bases every time that you're in front of somebody so that you're consistent in what you're reinforcing. So page seven, here are what I believe are the four essentials in order to have influence with them. One is they have to be fascinated with you. And one of the principles we'll talk about is the principle of fascination. But there is a difference between fascination and interest. And an example I give you is the way serial fiction writers are able to get people so fascinated with their characters that people are demanding another book about that character. Dean Kootz, who's like a Stephen King kind of writer, if you don't know, was interviewed on a radio show. I had on driving over here today. And he said that he never intended to go back to the same character more than once, because he has a low boredom threshold, and really didn't want to be churning out story number 32 about the same character or collection of characters. Never thought he'd want to do that. And he's got this character called Odd Thomas. And he's now on the fifth book coming out. And they have a couple hundred thousand copies preordered. And it hadn't even, I mean, it's like he's writing it. And it's preordered at Amazon. And he gets mail. And people like are demanding the next story about this character. And so that level of fascination goes far beyond interest. These people are actually waking up in the morning and wondering, what the hell is this Odd Thomas guy doing this week? And that really is, I think, important. And so we're going to talk about the principle of fascination. They have to feel that they share common values with you. Very, very hard to influence people who feel they have dramatically different values. We will see how possible it is to do that. In one example, has the Republican primaries play out. And we see whether or not Giuliani can survive. Iowa and New Hampshire long enough to get to some more moderate states. Because right now, you have a Republican front runner who has no value connection to the people he must get to vote for him. He has a legend connection, which we're going to talk about that. But he has zero values connection. There's like no match. And that's historically impossible to pull off. So that will be interesting. Third, they have to believe you care about them. Whether you do or not, and I pass no judgment on whether somebody does or doesn't, but they got to believe you do. They got to think it's far more than the money. And that there is personal concern that they do well. Now, in our businesses, if you actually had the level of personal concern that they do well, that they need to feel you have, you can't function in our businesses. Because we know 80%, 90% of them aren't going to do well because they aren't going to do anything. And so if you actually worried about them as individuals, Bill and Mary, or you worried statistically about who was getting results and who wasn't, you'll be in a nut farm or completely paralyzed and incapacitated in marketing what it is that you market. So it's essential you get over that. But on some level, you have to really care about the ones who do do well. And that has to communicate to everybody. Excuse me. And they must get value. By the way, the first time I heard that, I thought it was really hokey. Kavett Robert is the founder of the National Speaker's Association, deceased now. Kavett, interesting guy, worked for Elliott Ness in the Untouchables days in New York as a prosecutor and was sent to Arizona to retire for his health until he had like five years to live. And Kavett lived another 55 years or something. And he got in the burial insurance business and then became a sales trainer. And so one of Kavett's deals was they don't care how much you know until they know how much you care. And the first time I heard it, I'm like, eh. This is sales training by Hallmark Card. Did you know? And the longer I've been in our kinds of businesses, the more I realize he had it. And so often, we're trying to demonstrate knowledge and trying to demonstrate expertise. And they don't really care about that unless they actually believe you care about them on a personal and emotional level. Fourth, they have to get value from the relationship that they perceive they have with you. And it's very important to understand that that's value as they define it. Not value as you might define it. So how do you know if you're doing it right, which is an interesting place to start? And I've got five things for you to look for. One is that they tell your stories back to you and then tell you their same story. And so everybody in here does live events, right? At some point or another, you get face to face with your customers. Or you're on calls with them or whatever. You kind of know that you're getting this done if they're coming up to you and telling you one of your stories that you use and use a lot. And then telling you their version of the same experience. And so if they're coming up and telling you about your disease and then their disease and how they're handling it, or your dog and their cat or your kid and their kid, if they're telling that back to you, then they've bought into a great connection with you. And not only should they be telling you that, you ought to be getting mail that tells you that. And so if you're not getting that kind of personal fan mail that is relating stories from them comparable to your stories, you aren't connecting. So like I have a list, you guys are all familiar with them and I have it in here somewhere of like Dan's stories, right? And so I get a lot of, for example, I get a lot of bankruptcy mail. I get a lot of people telling me they're going into, they're coming out of, they're recovery from bankruptcy stories. I get a lot of car repossession stories. Usually same people, by the way. And every time something new gets added, then there's activity about that. So the divorce sparked all sorts of mail and storytelling to me in that category that, by the way, I never got before.
